title: Eight AI Forces
subtitle: The forces that are influencing AI model capabilities

callouts.label: R1 — Recursive Self-Improvement
callouts.hint: Warm orange icon on the upper-left showing two arrows chasing each other in a circle.
callouts.quiz_description: Each new model makes the lab's own engineers more productive. Those productive engineers then ship an even better next model — which makes them even more productive. The loop runs through human engineers, who are still in the driver's seat picking which experiments to try.
callouts.description: A reinforcing loop: a lab ships a model that is dramatically better at writing code; that model makes its own engineers more productive; productive engineers ship the next model faster; the next model is even better. In CLD terms: Model Capability → Code Generation Quality → R&D Productivity → Model Capability. This loop is real and already operating at every frontier lab to some degree — but it still routes through human engineers, which caps how fast it can spin.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Strong. Every frontier lab uses its own model in its own pipeline. The runaway story tells you R1 alone predicts a single winner — but R1 is one loop in a system of nine. Reading it in isolation is the classic mistake.

callouts.label: R2 — Autonomous Research
callouts.hint: Red icon on the left showing a stylized atom with three intersecting orbits.
callouts.quiz_description: The model itself decides which experiments to run, runs them, and interprets the results. Humans drop out of the inner loop entirely. Iteration speed stops being limited by how fast scientists can think. This is the supercritical step that, if it ever closes, lets a leader pull away faster than competitors can read the papers.
callouts.description: The supercritical loop. R1 still routes through humans — the model writes code, but humans pick the experiments. R2 is what happens when the model designs, runs, and interprets its OWN ML experiments. The research loop closes inside the company and decouples from human pace. If R2 ever becomes self-sustaining, it can iterate faster than competitors can read the papers.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Emerging — real but not yet self-sustaining. This is the single most important variable in the entire analysis. A model genuinely 10× ahead at code generation matters not because of R1, but because it would be the first credible candidate to close R2.

callouts.label: R3 — Capital → Compute
callouts.hint: Deep red icon on the left showing a stack of coins next to a small microchip.
callouts.quiz_description: Better models win market share. Share turns into revenue and strategic investment from deep-pocketed partners. That money buys GPUs and electricity, which trains the next round of better models. Google's $40B commitment to Anthropic is the loudest current example.
callouts.description: The financial loop. Better models win share, share becomes revenue and strategic investment, capital buys compute, compute trains better models. Google's commitment of up to $40B to Anthropic is exactly this loop being amplified — not by organic revenue, but by a strategic partner who wants Anthropic to keep pace with OpenAI and xAI on training scale. A persistent capital advantage weakens the compute constraint (B1) for the leader specifically while it keeps biting everyone else.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Strong, asymmetric. The Google–Anthropic deal makes R3 highly asymmetric. That asymmetry is how oligopoly tips toward duopoly, and duopoly toward monopoly.

callouts.label: R4 — Data Flywheel
callouts.hint: Amber icon on the lower-left showing a thick circular flywheel with motion lines.
callouts.quiz_description: Real users and deployed agents quietly generate training signal — preference clicks, trace logs, error corrections — that nobody outside the deploying company can see. Public benchmarks plateau, but this private signal keeps growing. A competitor with the same architecture cannot reproduce the trail.
callouts.description: The lock-in loop. Real users, agents, and enterprise integrations produce a kind of training signal that simply does not exist outside the company that owns the deployment — agent traces, preference signal, error corrections. Public benchmarks plateau; private data flywheels do not. R4 is the most plausible structural reason a single winner could emerge: even if a competitor matches the architecture, they cannot match the data trail.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Strong, accelerating. Agent traces are creating private signal that did not exist 18 months ago. R4 is structurally the same shape as the classic AI Flywheel, but the unit changed: not 'user clicks improve recommendations,' but 'agent traces improve the next frontier model.'

callouts.label: B1 — Compute Constraint
callouts.hint: Steel-blue icon on the upper-right showing a lightning bolt inside a square outline.
callouts.quiz_description: A physical limit. Pushing the frontier means more chips, more datacenters, and electricity contracts measured in gigawatts. Chip supply, land, water, and power contracts cannot scale on the same exponential as the loss curve. This is the single biggest reason runaway self-improvement has not yet gone vertical.
callouts.description: The physical balancing force. Pushing the frontier raises the compute bill. Compute is constrained by chip supply, datacenter buildouts, and electricity contracts measured in gigawatts. B1 slows everyone, including the leader, and is the single biggest reason recursive self-improvement has not yet gone vertical. R3 weakens B1 for capital-rich players — but does not eliminate it.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Very strong. Power and chip supply are still the binding constraint. Watch for compute-access asymmetry that survives a market downturn — that's the signal R3 has truly broken B1 for the leader.

callouts.label: B2 — Evaluation Bottleneck
callouts.hint: Teal icon on the right showing a magnifying glass hovering over a short checklist.
callouts.quiz_description: As models get more capable, knowing whether the new one is actually better than the old one becomes harder. Benchmarks get gamed, training sets get contaminated, reward signals get hacked, and regressions hide in plain sight. You cannot improve a model faster than you can measure it.
callouts.description: As models get more capable, they get harder to evaluate. Benchmark gaming, contamination, reward hacking, and hard-to-detect regressions all compound. The system can't reliably tell whether the new model is actually better, which means improvement gets noisier and slower at exactly the moment the runaway loop wants it to be cleaner and faster. The most underrated loop by people excited about R2: you cannot self-improve faster than you can self-evaluate.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Very strong. Reliable measurement is harder, not easier, year over year. If R2 ever closes, B2 is the last balancing loop standing — and it is not enough on its own.

callouts.label: B3 — Diffusion / Fast-Follow
callouts.hint: Emerald-green icon on the right showing three small stacked rectangles fanning outward.
callouts.quiz_description: Ideas keep escaping. Papers get published, employees switch labs, system prompts leak, and competitors reverse-engineer inference traces. The leader's relative advantage decays continuously, and the faster they move, the more visible the breakthrough — and the faster the rest of the field copies it.
callouts.description: Even when AI writes most of the code, ideas still propagate. Papers leak, employees move, prompts get exfiltrated, inference traces get reverse-engineered. The leader's relative advantage decays continuously. The faster the leader moves, the more visible the breakthrough, the faster competitors copy it. B3 is why every previous 'permanent moat' claim in software has eventually collapsed.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Weakening. Talent mobility helps competitors, but post-training tricks now leak more slowly than the underlying architectures did a few years ago.

callouts.label: B4 — Cost-Performance Friction
callouts.hint: Deep cyan icon on the lower-right showing a small balance scale with two pans.
callouts.quiz_description: The smartest model is rarely the one customers actually run in production. As capability rises, so does the per-token bill. Most agents quietly route to a cheaper model that is 'good enough,' starving the frontier model of the very deployment data it needs. The market keeps choosing affordable competence over expensive brilliance.
callouts.description: The frontier model is not always the most-used model. As capability rises, so does inference cost. Most production agents will route to a cheaper model that is 'good enough,' which chokes R4 (the data flywheel) for the leader. B4 is why the cheapest competent model often beats the smartest expensive one in market terms — a real-world counterweight to the runaway hypothesis.
callouts.ap_tip: Status (May 2026): Strong. Cheap-and-good models still dominate production deployment volume. A fifth balancing force worth naming alongside B4: market fragmentation by use case (regulated industries, sovereign-AI requirements, on-device constraints) — a field of 'good enough specialists' is itself a structural defense against a single global winner.
